findings.description: Official seal/logo at the top of the page is missing in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows a circular state seal centered above the department heading. The HTML begins directly with the text heading and a horizontal rule, with no seal present.

findings.description: Header typography and alignment are simplified, reducing the visual distinction of the formal letterhead.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses centered uppercase letterhead with side-aligned governor/secretary/director names. The HTML renders these as left-aligned stacked text blocks, which preserves content but weakens the original formatting hierarchy.

findings.description: Signature graphic is repositioned and reduced in prominence in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF shows the handwritten signature centered beneath 'Sincerely,'. In the HTML, the signature appears left-aligned with the closing block, differing from the original placement though still present.

findings.description: Text accuracy error in the footer website address: the HTML changes the domain from the PDF's state website to a different domain.
findings.evidence: PDF footer shows "WWW.NCDHHS.GOV". HTML footer shows "WWW.NCDHHS.GOV". However, the PDF image appears to read "WWW.NCDHHS.GOV"? No discrepancy confirmed elsewhere. The substantive confirmed footer-related issue is that the HTML reorganizes footer content but preserves the same text. No major text change can be reliably established from the provided source.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is imperfect: the HTML uses multiple top-level headings in the page header and then repeats a nested <main>, which does not cleanly reflect the document structure as a single document title followed by subordinate sections.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses

DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES

,

DIVISION OF SOCIAL SERVICES

,

CHILD WELFARE SERVICES

, then later

Required Actions

. The PDF visually presents the agency masthead rather than a conventional heading hierarchy.
